title: An Embedding Learning Framework for Numerical Features in CTR Prediction (AutoDis)
authors: Huifeng Guo, Bo Chen, Ruiming Tang, Weinan Zhang, Zhenguo Li, Xiuqiang He (Huawei Noah's Ark Lab; Shanghai Jiao Tong University)
tokenization_method: AutoDis: a per-field numerical-feature embedding framework built from three modules. (1) Meta-Embeddings ME_j in R^{H_j x d}: a small bank of H_j shared latent 'bucket' embeddings per numerical field (NOT per value). (2) Automatic Discretization d_Auto_j(.): a differentiable two-layer leaky-ReLU MLP with skip-connection that maps scalar x_j to H_j projection scores ex_j = W_j*LeakyReLU(w_j*x_j) + alpha*LeakyReLU(w_j*x_j), then a temperature-tau softmax turns them into a soft assignment distribution bx_j over the H_j meta-embeddings. (3) Aggregation f(.): combines the meta-embeddings using the soft scores as weights. The final feature embedding e_j = sum_h bx_j^h * ME_j^h (Weighted-Average). Net effect: a fully end-to-end, differentiable soft replacement for hand-designed numerical-feature discretization, jointly trained with the CTR model's loss.
feature_types_handled: numerical (continuous) only — the core contribution. Categorical fields keep standard learned per-category embedding tables (unchanged, not a contribution). No temporal modeling, no explicit missing-value mechanism.
token_granularity: per-field learned code (soft codebook lookup): one embedding vector e_j per numerical field, produced as a soft-weighted mixture over H_j field-specific meta-embeddings (codes). Conceptually a differentiable soft-VQ over a tiny per-field codebook of size H_j (20-40), not a one-token-per-value scheme.
numerical_strategy: Soft automatic discretization (target-driven, learned end-to-end). The scalar x_j is projected by a 2-layer leaky-ReLU net with skip-connection to H_j bucket scores, softmaxed with a temperature coefficient tau into a soft assignment over H_j meta-embeddings, then aggregated by weighted-average. tau can be a single learned global value OR generated per-instance by a small adaptive temperature network conditioned on the feature value and a global field statistic (the latter wins). Contrasts with hard discretization (EDD/LD/TD) by being differentiable and jointly optimized with the CTR objective.
categorical_strategy: Standard learned embedding table per categorical field; inherited from the CTR backbone, not modified.
high_cardinality_handling: None specific. Categorical handling is conventional embedding-table lookup; high-cardinality is not the paper's concern.
temporal_handling: none — static CTR logs (Criteo/Avazu-style i.i.d. impressions); no time-varying feature modeling.
scales_to_many_features: Designed to be parameter-light per numerical field: cost is O(H_j*d) meta-embeddings + a tiny H_j x H_j discretization net per field, with H_j fixed at 20 (Criteo) / 40 (AutoML), so per-field params are CONSTANT in the number of distinct values (unlike a full hard-discretization embedding table that scales with bucket count, or per-value embeddings). Authors stress 'model complexity is highly controllable, making our method scalable' and report inference overhead comparable to EDD with no offline feature-engineering step. NOT stress-tested on 70+-heterogeneous mixed feature sets in the topic's sense; CTR fields are mostly low-cardinality categorical + a handful of numerical.
downstream_model: Backbone-agnostic plug-in for deep CTR models. Demonstrated inside FNN, Wide&Deep, DeepFM, DCN, IPNN (and a YouTube-DNN / DLRM 'no-embedding' baseline). DeepFM-AutoDis is the headline configuration. Consumes flat concatenated field embeddings feeding an MLP / FM / cross network.
pretraining_objective: none — fully supervised, trained end-to-end with the CTR binary cross-entropy (LogLoss) objective. The discretization is learned jointly (no separate pretraining or offline bin-fitting step).
key_results: Public Criteo + private AutoML + Industrial datasets. Overall (DeepFM backbone): AutoDis AUC 0.8152 / 0.7562 / 0.7281 vs best discretization baseline (LD/TD ~0.8138 / 0.7545 / 0.7265), all improvements significant (p<0.05). % AUC improvement 0.17-0.23% (large in CTR where 0.1% AUC is meaningful). Compatibility study: AutoDis lifts every backbone (FNN, Wide&Deep, DeepFM, DCN, IPNN) over its field-embedding baseline. Aggregation ablation (Criteo AUC): Weighted-Average 0.7562 > Top-K-Sum 0.7549 > Max-Pooling 0.7515 — confirming soft > hard selection. Adaptive per-instance temperature beats the best searched global tau (~1e-5 Criteo, ~4e-3 AutoML). Online A/B: +2.1% CTR, +2.7% eCPM.
baselines_compared: Numerical-handling baselines: No-Embedding (raw normalized scalar into DNN; YouTube-DNN, DLRM); Field Embedding (FE, single shared field vector scaled by value); hard discretization EDD (equal-distance), LD (logarithm), TD (tree-based). Backbones: FNN, Wide&Deep, DeepFM, DCN, IPNN. Note: the later Gorishniy et al. 2022 numerical-embeddings paper benchmarks AutoDis as a baseline and finds PLE/PLR generally stronger on GBDT-friendly tabular data.
transferable_idea: Soft, per-field learned discretization over a TINY shared codebook (H=20-40 meta-embeddings), produced by a differentiable scorer + temperature-softmax + weighted-average — giving a continuous, end-to-end, parameter-bounded numerical tokenizer that avoids the Similar-value-But-Dissimilar-embedding (SBD) and Dissimilar-value-But-Same-embedding (DBS) artefacts of hard binning. For a 70+-feature tokenizer this is a cheap, scalable alternative to per-value or large-bin embedding tables for the numerical half, and the soft-codebook view bridges to RQ-VAE/semantic-ID discretization on the VQ route.
limitations: (1) Numerical-only; no categorical, temporal, or missing-value innovation. (2) Evaluated only on CTR-style data (low-cardinality categorical + few numerical), not on 70+-heterogeneous mixed tabular; scaling claim is about per-field param bound, not demonstrated on many numerical features. (3) Absolute AUC gains are small (0.17-0.23%) — meaningful for industrial CTR but modest as a general tabular method. (4) Per-field embedding is still independent (no cross-field sharing), and H_j must be set per dataset. (5) Subsequent benchmarks (Gorishniy 2022) find AutoDis weaker than PLE/PLR on general GBDT-friendly tabular tasks.
pitfalls: (1) The 'scalable' claim is about a constant per-field parameter budget, NOT about handling many features or high attention cost — do not read it as a many-feature scaling result. (2) Soft assignment with a poorly-set temperature collapses either to near-uniform mixing (loses resolution) or to argmax (degenerates to hard discretization, reintroducing SBD/DBS) — tau is load-bearing and the global optimum varies wildly across datasets (1e-5 vs 4e-3), which is why the paper needs an adaptive-tau network. (3) Gains are CTR-dataset-specific; the method has been beaten on broader tabular benchmarks, so do not treat AutoDis as the default numerical tokenizer outside CTR without re-benchmarking. (4) Meta-embeddings are field-shared, so two numerically distant values can still alias onto the same code if H_j is too small — H_j is a quality knob, not free.
relevance_to_topic: Canonical 'learned/soft discretization' entry for the discretization-vq route: shows how to turn a continuous feature into a differentiable soft lookup over a small per-field codebook, end-to-end with the task loss, at bounded parameter cost — directly reusable as the numerical half of a 70+-mixed-feature tokenizer and a conceptual bridge to VQ/RQ-VAE semantic IDs.
code_available: no official public repo found from the authors (Huawei Noah's Ark internal); the mechanism is reimplemented in third-party CTR libraries (e.g. FuxiCTR / community DeepCTR variants) and benchmarked as a baseline in the rtdl numerical-embeddings codebase.
